text: and conclusive writing, Plato re- grounds his entire epistemology in children’s play: a healthy culture of the polis can only grow out of the balanced and musical play of children. But here they must be guided, because children, as we know, may take pleasure both in what is good and what is bad. Children, says Plato, must learn to take pleasure in what is good. That is the role of education, and that is the touchstone for Plato’s mature 2 On the role of liminality and the trickster’s game in education, and the formation of the participant as a ‘critical outsider’ (here with reference to introductory texts in sociology), see Boland 2010. Liminality and the Modern 144 political philosophy. A sense for the good, the just and the beautiful must develop together. The necessary affinities that (should) exist in our attitudes to the playful and the upbringing of children are again driven home by Greek etymology, in the tight connections between child (pais), playing (paidia) and culture (paideia). The laws, says Plato, are based on harmonious play; and the laws, the nomoi, are themselves like music. The fact that we have forgotten this touchstone does not diminish its relevance; quite the contrary. In what follows, it will of course be impossible to engage with the details of today’s enormous gambling or gaming industry in all its ordinary, pathological dimensions. There are entire libraries dedicated to the phenomenon of gambling, which is far from strange, considering the fact that it is one of the world’s biggest industries. We live in a world of constant gaming and constant gambling; that is the way it is, and it needs no further comment. What will be suggested is that here also, the diagnostic term proposed in previous chapters comes into its own, for this is one more sign that we are evidently plunging into an abyss of permanent liminality. But how did this happen? On the Religious Origins of ‘Games’ of Chance It is important to start with the fact that games of chance are not new to human beings, and as such do not belong specifically to modernity. Games of chance were up until very recently not at all related to money. Alfred Kroeber (1920) noticed that gambling-like activities took place even among peoples who have no notion of personal wealth. In his work on games among the Californian Indians, he included games of chance as one of five main types of game. Kroeber also noticed something quite crucial about the spread of gambling: not that it spread via specific games that were adopted across cultures, but that what was spread was much more like an attitude, an ethos – or, what, following Weber, we might also call a gambling ‘spirit’. It is this hidden spirit of the modern that needs to be retrieved. Archaeologists have found six-sided dice from around 3000 bc in present-day Iraq. And there is no doubt that four-sided dice existed for millennia before that. It is difficult to say with certainty how such dice were used 5000 years ago, but moving just a little closer to the present this difficulty disappears: devices used today in games of chance were originally used for making decisions during religious rituals (Brenner and Brenner 1990: 1). Drawing lots was used as a method to discover God’s will. One famous example is found in Leviticus (16: 6–10), in the context of the The Day of Atonement, where it reads: 6 Aaron is to offer the bull for his own sin offering to make atonement for himself and his household. 7 Then he is to take the two goats and present them before the L ord at the entrance to the tent of meeting. 8 He is to cast lots for the two goats – one lot for the L ord and the other for the scapegoat. 9 Aaron shall bring Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 145 the goat whose lot falls to the L ord and sacrifice it for a sin offering. 10 But the goat chosen by lot as the scapegoat shall be presented alive before the L ord to be used for making atonement by sending it into the wilderness as a scapegoat. 3 There are plenty of other examples in the Old Testament. Leaders and office holders were evidently appointed by drawing lots, as when Saul was selected to be King of Israel. When Jonah was thrown into the sea, it was because the lot fell on him, and so on. Lottery practices are widespread in a variety of cultures of which we have record. Throughout the world, and from the smallest to the most complex of societies, people have been using all sorts of devices (stones, sticks, leaves, arrows) to cast lots in bringing about a decision. In the Iliad, Zeus used a sacred balance to decide whether the Trojans or the Greeks would win the battle. In Greek mythology, the Gods cast lots to divide the universe among them, and that is how Zeus got the sky, Poseidon the seas and Hades the underworld. Hades lost in the lottery. However, and this is important to stress, casting lots in all these contexts was about making decisions in matters of religion, politics and law. And for the involved persons, such decisions were probably never really perceived as involving ‘chance’ at all, at least not as we have come to understand that term today; rather, casting lots was simply a modality or technique to find or bring about the will of the higher powers. The result of casting the lot was a technical means of uncovering destiny – and in fact, we still use the word ‘lot’ as synonymous with ‘destiny’. The notion of ‘pure chance’ is arguably only possible within a thoroughly disenchanted universe, as Dostoyevsky captured so tellingly in his novel, The Gambler (see Scanlan 2009). Therefore, the introduction of institutionalized, money-oriented games of chance needs to be accounted for. Anthropological Foundations and
